metadata.summary: Make JavaScript SPAs indexable: SSR/SSG for first paint, per-page meta + OG, JSON-LD structured data, crawlable links, sitemap, and Core Web Vitals.

content.title: SEO for SPAs
content.summary: A methodology for making JavaScript Single Page Applications indexable by search engines. Covers server-side rendering for first paint, per-page meta tag management, JSON-LD structured data, crawlable link patterns, sitemap generation, and Core Web Vitals performance.

why.p: Search engines historically struggled with client-rendered SPAs because crawlers see an empty HTML shell until JS executes. SSR ensures critical content appears in the initial HTML response; without it, Google may index blank pages or rank content poorly. Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID) are ranking signals — a SPA that loads slowly loses rank even if content is present.

list.item: Migrating a CSR-only React/Vue/Angular app to SSR/SSG (Next.js, Nuxt, Remix, Angular Universal) for indexable content.
Adding per-route meta + Open Graph + Twitter Card + canonical tags via Next `<Head>` / Metadata API or `react-helmet-async`.
Implementing JSON-LD structured data for Product, Article, FAQ, BreadcrumbList, Organization.
Generating `sitemap.xml` and `robots.txt` from data, plus hreflang for multi-locale apps.
Auditing Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) — the ranking-relevant subset of Lighthouse.
Internal app behind auth — search engines can't reach it; SEO budget is wasted.
Pure marketing-static site already on Astro / Gatsby / 11ty — those generate static HTML by default and most "SEO for SPA" patterns don't apply.
The site is on Cloudflare Pages with full prerendering already configured — additional SSR plumbing is duplicate work.
Next.js App Router replaced `<Head>` with the `metadata` export. Agents copy Pages Router examples and ship duplicate `<head>` tags.
Dynamic OG images: building one per request from `@vercel/og` is fine on Vercel/Edge; on a self-hosted Node server it eats CPU. Cache with `Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable`.
Googlebot now renders JS, but rendering is delayed and rate-limited. Critical content must be in the SSR HTML, not lazy-mounted.
Canonical wars: `canonical` pointing to a URL that 301s to another canonical creates an indexing loop — easy to introduce via locale prefixes.
`react-helmet` (legacy) is no longer maintained and has SSR memory leaks; use `react-helmet-async`.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Treat SEO as a per-route task. Subagent reads the route, identifies the content type (Product, Article, etc.), and emits: (1) meta tags, (2) JSON-LD, (3) canonical, (4) sitemap entry, (5) Open Graph image source. Use Lighthouse (mobile preset), `linkinator` for broken links, and Google's Rich Results Test API as oracles. Keep humans in the loop for keyword strategy and copy — those are content decisions, not engineering.

list.item: `faion-feature-executor` — per-route SEO retrofits with Lighthouse + structured-data validation gates.
`faion-sdd-executor-agent` — full-site SEO migrations (CSR → SSR) span spec, design (URL structure, sitemap), implementation, and tests.

section.prompt: For `/products/[slug]`, generate the Next.js App Router `generateMetadata` export and a Product JSON-LD `<script>`. Pull data via `getProduct(slug)`. Include canonical, OG, Twitter, hreflang for `en` and `uk`.
Audit Lighthouse SEO + Best Practices on `https://staging.example.com/products/widget`. List failing audits with file paths and proposed fixes; do not edit yet.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: CLI tools used in this methodology:

list.item: `npx lighthouse <url> --preset=desktop --only-categories=seo,performance` — SEO + CWV audit (https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/).
`pnpm dlx @lhci/cli autorun` — Lighthouse CI per PR (https://github.com/GoogleChrome/lighthouse-ci).
`npx linkinator https://example.com --recurse` — broken-link crawler for sitemap validation (https://github.com/JustinBeckwith/linkinator).
`pnpm add -D next-sitemap` — generate `sitemap.xml` + `robots.txt` from Next routes (https://github.com/iamvishnusankar/next-sitemap).
`npx schema-dts-gen` — type-safe JSON-LD via TypeScript (https://github.com/google/schema-dts).
`pnpm dlx @vercel/og` — programmatic OG image generation (https://vercel.com/docs/functions/og-image-generation).
Google Search Console (SaaS, partial REST API) — Coverage, Core Web Vitals, sitemap submit; agents can pull index status.
Google Rich Results Test (SaaS, web + URL inspection API) — Validate JSON-LD; treat as CI oracle for structured data.
Bing Webmaster Tools (SaaS, REST API) — Often forgotten; lower traffic but indexes faster for small sites.
Ahrefs / Semrush (SaaS, partial REST API, paid) — Backlinks + keyword data; out of scope for code agents.
Schema.org validator (OSS) — https://validator.schema.org/ — wrap with curl.
WebPageTest (SaaS + OSS, REST) — CWV testing under throttled networks.

section.name: core-rules
section.p: Concrete rules for SSR, meta tags, canonical URLs, and crawlable link structure in Single Page Applications.

rule.statement: Render critical page content in the initial HTML response — never rely on client-side JS for content that must be indexed.
rule.rationale: Search engine crawlers may not execute JavaScript, or may index the page before hydration completes. Content absent from the initial HTML is at risk of not being indexed.

rule.statement: Use SSR or SSG for all public-facing pages; defer CSR-only rendering to authenticated or non-indexed routes.
rule.rationale: Next.js getStaticProps/getStaticPaths and ISR (revalidate) provide SSG with on-demand regeneration — the best performance/freshness trade-off for most content sites.

example.title: Next.js page with ISR — content in initial HTML, revalidated every hour

antipattern.description: Fetching product data in useEffect and rendering into DOM after hydration.
antipattern.reason: The initial HTML contains no product content — crawlers may index an empty shell.

rule.statement: Every public page must have a unique title (under 60 chars), meta description (under 160 chars), canonical URL, Open Graph tags, and Twitter Card tags.
rule.rationale: Missing or duplicate meta tags cause indexing conflicts; Open Graph tags control how the page appears when shared on social platforms.

rule.statement: Set the canonical URL to the definitive version of the page URL to prevent duplicate content penalties.
rule.rationale: Query parameters (e.g. ?ref=email, ?page=1) can create duplicate URL variants; canonical signals the preferred version to crawlers.

example.title: Next.js Head with full meta tag set for a product page
example.code: <Head> <title>{product.name} | Example Store</title> <meta name="description" content={product.description.slice(0, 160)} /> <link rel="canonical" href={`https://example.com/products/${product.id}`} /> <meta property="og:type" content="product" /> <meta property="og:title" content={product.name} /> <meta property="og:description" content={product.description} /> <meta property="og:image" content={product.image} /> <meta property="og:url" content={`https://example.com/products/${product.id}`} /> <meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image" /> </Head>

antipattern.description: A single static title and description shared across all pages of a SPA.
antipattern.reason: Every page gets the same snippet in search results; click-through rate drops and duplicate content is flagged.

rule.statement: All navigable links must use anchor tags with a valid href attribute — never onClick-only divs or buttons for navigation.
rule.rationale: Crawlers follow href links; onClick handlers that call router.push() are invisible to bots and create orphaned pages that receive no link equity.

example.title: Crawlable Next.js Link + external anchor
onClick-only div for navigation
example.note: No href — invisible to search engine crawlers.

section.name: structured-data
section.p: Schema.org JSON-LD patterns for Product, Article, and Breadcrumb types, injected via a shared StructuredData component.

rule.statement: Inject JSON-LD structured data via a script tag with type="application/ld+json" inside the document head on every page that benefits from rich results.
rule.rationale: JSON-LD is Google's recommended format — it is decoupled from the DOM, easy to validate with Google's Rich Results Test, and does not require modifying HTML markup.

rule.statement: Product pages must include name, description, image, sku, brand, offers (with priceCurrency, price, availability URL, seller), and aggregateRating when reviews exist.
rule.rationale: Complete Product schema enables Google Shopping rich results and product carousels in SERPs, increasing click-through rate for e-commerce pages.

example.title: Product JSON-LD schema

rule.statement: Article pages must include headline, description, image, datePublished, dateModified, author (Person), publisher (Organization with logo), and mainEntityOfPage. Breadcrumb structured data must mirror the visible breadcrumb component.
rule.rationale: Article schema enables AMP and Top Stories rich results. BreadcrumbList schema provides path context in SERPs and must match the visible navigation to pass Google validation.

example.title: BreadcrumbList JSON-LD
example.note: Must match the rendered nav breadcrumb exactly.

antipattern.description: Breadcrumb schema that diverges from the visible breadcrumb navigation.
antipattern.reason: Google validates schema against rendered content; mismatches can result in a manual action or rich result loss.

section.name: performance
section.p: Next.js performance optimisations for LCP/CLS/FID, image handling, lazy loading, and automated sitemap + robots.txt generation.

rule.statement: Mark the Largest Contentful Paint image with priority={true} on the Next.js Image component. Never defer above-the-fold images.
rule.rationale: LCP is a direct ranking signal. The LCP element is typically the hero image or product photo; loading it eagerly instead of lazily is the single highest-impact LCP optimisation.

rule.statement: Reserve space for all images using explicit width and height (or aspect ratio) to avoid Cumulative Layout Shift.
rule.rationale: CLS penalises pages where content shifts as images load. Reserving dimensions prevents reflow.

rule.statement: Lazy-load non-critical components using next/dynamic with ssr: false only when the component is genuinely below-the-fold and not required for indexing.
rule.rationale: CSR-only components are invisible to crawlers; use this only for widgets (comments, analytics) that do not carry indexable content.

example.title: LCP image priority + below-fold dynamic widget

rule.statement: Generate sitemap.xml and robots.txt at build time. Include all public static pages, dynamic content URLs (products, posts), and exclude admin, API, and private routes.
rule.rationale: Sitemaps accelerate crawl discovery; robots.txt prevents crawl budget waste on non-indexable routes like /admin and /api.

example.title: Robots.txt with sitemap pointer + blocked admin/API
example.code: User-agent: * Allow: / Sitemap: https://example.com/sitemap.xml Disallow: /admin/ Disallow: /api/

rule.statement: Enable image optimisation with modern formats (avif, webp), set long-lived Cache-Control headers for static assets, and disable production source maps to reduce bundle size.
rule.rationale: AVIF/WebP reduces image payload by 20-50% vs JPEG; immutable cache headers prevent unnecessary revalidation; source maps in production unnecessarily double the download size.

section.name: minimal-sitemap-snippet
section.p: Minimal sitemap script for static export (canonical short form):

list.item: Critical content must be in the SSR HTML, not appended via `useEffect`. Test with `curl <url> | grep <expected-text>`.
One canonical per page. If you serve trailing-slash and non-trailing-slash variants, redirect 301 to the canonical form.
JSON-LD goes in `<head>` (or just before `</body>`); place exactly one block per type per page. Multiple Product blocks confuse Googlebot.
Keep meta description under 160 chars and unique per page; avoid templated "Welcome to X" descriptions.
Set `Cache-Control` on OG images aggressively — they get crawled from Slack/Twitter/Facebook bots, all of which hit the URL hard.
For multi-locale, pair `hreflang` tags with `<link rel="alternate">` and a sitemap `<xhtml:link>` block; tag mismatches are the #1 indexing bug.
Agents add `<title>` both via `metadata` export and a manual `<head>` element in a layout — Next dedupes inconsistently and one wins per route.
Generated JSON-LD often hard-codes `@id` to a placeholder URL; Search Console flags as "missing `@id`" or duplicate.
LLMs use `next/image` then forget the explicit `width`/`height`, causing CLS regressions that tank the SEO score.
For dynamic OG images, agents fetch fonts inside the handler on every request; use the prebuilt edge runtime + `fetch` with cache headers.
`robots.txt` regenerated by an agent often blocks `/api/` and accidentally `/api/og/*` — OG images stop appearing on social.
Generated `sitemap.xml` includes draft / private routes when the route function returns all DB rows; filter by published flag.

template.purpose: Reusable SEO head component for React Helmet or Next.js Head.
